bytes.topic: EVM Architecture — opcodes, gas model, stack-based execution, memory vs storage vs calldata
bytes.title: Base EVM Architecture — Understanding Opcodes, Gas Model, and Data Storage
bytes.body: Base's EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine) architecture relies on a stack-based execution model, using opcodes to perform operations. The gas model determines the cost of each operation, with gas prices influencing transaction fees. Data is stored in three primary locations: memory (volatile, used for temporary computations), storage (non-volatile, used for contract state), and calldata (immutable, used for function arguments). Understanding these components is crucial for optimizing Base smart contracts. The `base` library provides APIs to interact with the EVM, such as `base.eth.getTransactionCount()` to retrieve the number of transactions sent from a given address.

bytes.topic: Ethereum — accounts, transactions, block structure, consensus (PoS post-merge)
bytes.title: Building Ethereum Accounts and Transactions on Base
bytes.body: Base provides a robust set of APIs for interacting with the Ethereum blockchain. To create an Ethereum account on Base, you can use the `eth_account` module. This module allows you to generate new accounts, sign transactions, and verify signatures. When building transactions, you'll need to specify the `from` address, `to` address, `value`, `gas`, and `gasPrice`. The `eth_transaction` module on Base handles the low-level details of transaction construction. Post-merge, Ethereum has transitioned to a Proof of Stake (PoS) consensus algorithm, which is supported by Base's `eth_consensus` module.

bytes.topic: Gas & Fees — gas limit, base fee, priority fee (EIP-1559), gas optimization
bytes.title: Optimizing Gas Costs with Base EVM

bytes.topic: Layer 2 Scaling — why L1 is expensive, rollup approach, optimistic vs ZK
bytes.title: BASE L2 Scaling — Rollup and Optimistic vs ZK
bytes.body: BASE's EVM implementation struggles with high gas prices due to its Layer 1 (L1) nature. Rollup solutions like Optimism and zk-Rollup alleviate this issue by batching and compressing transactions off-chain. Optimism uses a optimistic fraud-proofs (oFp) approach, which relies on a validator network to check on-chain for any discrepancies. zk-Rollup, on the other hand, utilizes zero-knowledge proofs (ZKP) to verify transactions without exposing any sensitive information. Both methods enable BASE to process more transactions per block, reducing the congestion and gas costs.

bytes.topic: Optimistic Rollups — fraud proofs, challenge period, withdrawal delay (Arbitrum, Optimism)
bytes.title: Optimistic Rollups on base: Understanding Fraud Proofs
bytes.body: Optimistic Rollups, like Arbitrum and Optimism, use a challenge period to detect potential fraud proofs. When a user submits a batch of transactions, a fraud proof is generated to ensure the transactions are valid. The challenge period is the time during which anyone can challenge the fraud proof. If a challenge is made, the batch is reverted and the challenge period is extended. For base, the challenge period is set to 2 minutes. Additionally, withdrawals are delayed for 10 minutes to prevent sudden large transfers. This delay allows for more time to detect and respond to potential fraud proofs.

bytes.topic: Polygon — PoS chain, AggLayer, zkEVM, bridging assets
bytes.title: Base on Polygon: Bridging Assets with AggLayer and zkEVM
bytes.body: To bridge assets from Polygon to Base, utilize the AggLayer contract. First, deploy the AggLayer contract on Base. Then, on Polygon, call the deposit function with the asset's address and the amount to bridge. The AggLayer on Base will then create a new asset bridge contract. Finally, use the transfer function on the bridge contract to transfer the assets from Polygon to Base. For more complex asset bridging, consider using Polygon's zkEVM, which provides a more efficient and private bridging mechanism.

bytes.topic: Bridges — locking/minting, canonical vs third-party bridges, bridge risks
bytes.title: base: Safe Bridge Implementation with Locking and Minting
bytes.body: When building bridges on base, you must consider locking and minting mechanisms. Locking ensures that assets are transferred from the source chain to the destination chain, while minting creates new assets on the destination chain. base provides the `Bridge` contract, which offers a locking mechanism with the `lock` function. To mint assets, use the `mint` function in the `Bridge` contract. However, third-party bridges can be more versatile, but they introduce additional risks. To mitigate these risks, use a canonical bridge that follows the base guidelines.

bytes.topic: Base — Coinbase's L2, OP Stack, Superchain vision
bytes.title: base: Leveraging OP Stack for Optimized Execution

bytes.topic: Transaction Lifecycle — mempool, ordering, finality, reorg risk
bytes.title: BASE Transaction Lifecycle — Mempool, Ordering, Finality, Reorg Risk
bytes.body: BASE transactions are processed through a mempool, which is a collection of unconfirmed transactions awaiting inclusion in a block. When a validator proposes a new block, it selects transactions from the mempool based on their priority (gas price and gas limit). The block is then broadcast to the network, and validators verify its transactions. Once a block is finalized, its transactions are considered irreversible. However, if a reorg occurs (a new block is added to the chain that invalidates previous blocks), unconfirmed transactions in the mempool may need to be re-propagated.
